claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is your child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, please tell me each of their ages (or their names and ages), and we can assess eligibility for each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, yes I'm looking to find out about Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 12, and Blake who is 6 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely to meet you! Thank you for providing those details. Let me assess Child Benefit eligibility for both **Alex (12 years old)** and **Blake (6 years old)**.

Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the next checks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This question is more relevant for older children, but I should still ask to be thorough:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (12 and 6), I'd expect they're not working, but please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh no, they're just kids! Neither of them work at all - they're at school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense - they're both at school and not working. That's a clear "yes" to this check. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means they're not personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit themselves. Given their ages, this would be very unlikely, but I just need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't get any benefits themselves. They're just kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly, that's what I'd expect for children their age. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good news - this check is about apprenticeships, which clearly doesn't apply to Alex and Blake since they're at school and are only 12 and 6 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check some details about you (the person claiming).

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

The most common situation is simply living in the UK. Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both children?**

You qualify as responsible if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with the children** (most common)
2. You **contribute to their upkeep** (paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. You **pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

Do Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives at home with me, but Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since the children have different living situations, I'll need to assess them separately.

**For Blake (6 years old):** Living with you means you clearly have responsibility - that's straightforward. ✓

**For Alex (12 years old):** Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask a follow-up question:

**Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep in any of these ways?**

- Paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent) towards their care
- This could include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs, presents
- Payments can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum
- Or paying maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do you financially contribute to Alex's care in any of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I help with clothes and school dinners for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. As long as your contributions towards Alex's clothes and school dinners are at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount, that counts as contributing to their upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good to know. For reference, your contributions to Alex should be at least equivalent to the Child Benefit rate. Since you're helping with clothes and school dinners, this likely qualifies as contributing to their upkeep.

Let me proceed with the assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question, especially for Alex's situation:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

- For **Blake** - Is there anyone else (e.g., a partner or family member) claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
- For **Alex** - Since Alex doesn't live with you, is the person Alex lives with claiming Child Benefit for them?

Only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child, so I need to check this for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Blake - well, Blake's other parent lives with Blake and they already claim Child Benefit for them.

For Alex - no, nobody else is claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being so clear about this - it's really helpful!

**For Alex (12 years old):** No one else is claiming, so we can continue with the assessment. ✓

**For Blake (6 years old):** This is more complicated. Since Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for Blake, only one person can receive it. 

I have a follow-up question about Blake:

**Has Blake's other parent agreed to transfer the Child Benefit claim to you, or is there a dispute about who should receive it?**

- If you've both agreed that **you** should receive the Child Benefit instead, the other parent can stop their claim and you can make a new one
- If there's a **dispute** about who should claim, HMRC will need to make a decision (usually the person the child lives with has priority)

What's the situation - is there an agreement, or is this disputed?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, there's no dispute - Blake lives with them, so they're the one who claims it. That makes sense.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That makes perfect sense. Since Blake lives with their other parent and they're already claiming Child Benefit, that's the correct arrangement - the person the child lives with typically has priority for the claim.

So let me continue the assessment just for **Alex**, since Blake is already covered by the other parent's claim.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, about Alex's current living situation:

**Where does Alex currently live?**

Is Alex:
- **In hospital** (long-term stay)
- **In local authority care** (looked after by the council)
- **Being fostered**
- **Living normally** (e.g., with family, the other parent, or another arrangement)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in hospital at the moment - they've been there for a few months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear Alex is in hospital - I hope they're getting good care and recovering well.

Since Alex has been in hospital for a few months, I need to check some details about the duration.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. There's a **12-week rule** for children in hospital that I need to check.

**How long has Alex been in hospital?**

- Child Benefit can continue for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If they've been in hospital for longer than 12 weeks, there are additional rules about whether you're still regularly spending money on Alex's needs

You mentioned "a few months" - could you tell me more specifically:
1. Roughly how many weeks has Alex been in hospital?
2. Has this been one continuous stay, or have there been breaks where Alex came home?

(Note: If Alex came home but returned to hospital within 28 days, those stays are added together for the 12-week calculation)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 12 weeks now - just the one stay, they haven't been home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks in one continuous stay, we're right at the limit. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question:

**Are you regularly spending money on Alex while they're in hospital?**

You mentioned earlier that you help with clothes and school dinners for Alex. Since Alex is currently in hospital, are you still regularly spending money on their needs? This could include things like:

- Clothes
- Treats or comfort items
- Travel costs to visit
- Any other regular spending for Alex's benefit

If you are regularly spending money on Alex, this can allow Child Benefit to continue even at or beyond the 12-week mark.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy their clothes, magazines, and favorite food.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - since you're regularly spending money on Alex (clothes, magazines, and food), you can continue to be eligible for Child Benefit even with the hospital stay. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost at the end of the assessment. One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex's hospital stay being funded through the NHS or private means, rather than being paid for by the local council?

(You generally cannot receive Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's accommodation or maintenance.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for anything - it's just the NHS hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's the final check complete. Since the council isn't paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance (it's just NHS care), you meet all the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! I've now completed the assessment and can give you the outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Assessment Complete 🎉

Here's a summary of your Child Benefit eligibility:

### Alex (12 years old) - ✅ ELIGIBLE

You **can** claim Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:
- Alex is under 16 years old
- You contribute to Alex's upkeep (clothes, school dinners)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Although Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, you're regularly spending money on them (clothes, magazines, favourite food), which keeps you eligible
- The hospital stay is NHS-funded, not council-funded

### Blake (6 years old) - ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE (for you)

You **cannot** claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit for them
- Blake lives with the other parent, giving them priority
- Only one person can receive Child Benefit per child

*Note: Blake IS receiving Child Benefit - just through the other parent's claim, which is correct.*

---

### Next Steps for Alex

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** (or adoption certificate if applicable)
4. **Be aware**: If you or a partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply - but you can still claim and receive the National Insurance credits

Payments are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receive certain benefits).

I hope Alex recovers well. Is there anything else you'd like to know about the Child Benefit process?

reasonings: Alex (12 years old) - ELIGIBLE: Alex is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester) and meet residency requirements. Although Alex doesn't live with you, you contribute to their upkeep by paying for clothes and school dinners. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on them (clothes, magazines, favourite food), which satisfies the exception to the 12-week hospital rule. The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation - it's NHS care. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (6 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE FOR YOU TO CLAIM: Blake is under 16 and would otherwise qualify for Child Benefit. However, Blake lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the other parent has priority as the person Blake lives with, you cannot make a separate claim for Blake. Blake IS covered by Child Benefit - just through the other parent's claim.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria - they're under 16, you contribute to their upkeep, and despite being in hospital for 12 weeks, your regular spending on Alex means you remain eligible. For Blake, Child Benefit is already being claimed by the other parent who Blake lives with, which is the correct arrangement. You should apply for Child Benefit for Alex only.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (12 years old) - ELIGIBLE: Alex is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK (Manchester) and meet residency requirements. Although Alex doesn't live with you, you contribute to their upkeep by paying for clothes and school dinners. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex has been in hospital for about 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on them (clothes, magazines, favourite food), which satisfies the exception to the 12-week hospital rule. The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation - it's NHS care. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (6 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE FOR YOU TO CLAIM: Blake is under 16 and would otherwise qualify for Child Benefit. However, Blake lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the other parent has priority as the person Blake lives with, you cannot make a separate claim for Blake. Blake IS covered by Child Benefit - just through the other parent's claim.

response.overall_reasoning: You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria - they're under 16, you contribute to their upkeep, and despite being in hospital for 12 weeks, your regular spending on Alex means you remain eligible. For Blake, Child Benefit is already being claimed by the other parent who Blake lives with, which is the correct arrangement. You should apply for Child Benefit for Alex only.
